subtopic_detail: Serotonin syndrome from SSRI plus tramadol
stem: A 35-year-old woman with major depressive disorder has been on sertraline 100 mg daily for 6 months. Her GP prescribed tramadol 50 mg four-times daily for back pain 36 hours ago. She now presents to the emergency department with fever, agitation, tremor, sweating and intermittent involuntary lower limb jerking. She has not started any other new medication and denies illicit substance use.

data_table.Vital signs: Temp 39.5 degrees Celsius, HR 130 sinus, BP 160/95, RR 24, SpO2 98% in room air
data_table.Examination: Agitated, mydriasis, hyperreflexia, sustained ankle clonus, tremor of outstretched hands, diaphoretic; no muscular rigidity
data_table.Investigations: CK 800, creatinine 95, sodium 138, potassium 4.0, glucose 6.2; ECG sinus tachycardia

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Stop both serotonergic agents, IV fluids and benzodiazepines, active cooling, and consider cyproheptadine if persistent
options.rationale: Serotonin syndrome from SSRI plus tramadol is managed by ceasing all serotonergic agents, supportive care with IV fluids, benzodiazepines for agitation and hyperthermia, active cooling and oral cyproheptadine 12 mg loading if symptoms persist. (Source: eTG Psychotropic: Serotonin syndrome)

options.text: IV dantrolene 2.5 mg/kg as first-line treatment for hyperthermia and muscle rigidity in this setting
options.rationale: Dantrolene is used in malignant hyperthermia and severe neuroleptic malignant syndrome; serotonin syndrome lacks rigidity and is managed differently. (Source: eTG Psychotropic: Serotonin syndrome)

options.text: IV bromocriptine 2.5 mg three times daily to reverse dopaminergic blockade and stabilise the syndrome
options.rationale: Bromocriptine is used in NMS where there is dopamine antagonism; it is not indicated in serotonin syndrome where the issue is excess serotonin. (Source: eTG Psychotropic: Serotonin syndrome)

options.text: Continue sertraline and add propranolol 40 mg three times daily for ongoing autonomic instability
options.rationale: Continuing the serotonergic drug perpetuates the syndrome; cessation of all serotonergic agents is essential to recovery. (Source: AMH: Selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors)

options.text: Empiric IV ceftriaxone and vancomycin for presumed sepsis with confirmatory blood cultures pending
options.rationale: Sepsis is a differential and cultures should be sent but the clinical syndrome (clonus, hyperreflexia, mydriasis, hyperthermia, serotonergic drug exposure) fits serotonin toxicity, which is treated by drug cessation and supportive care. (Source: eTG Psychotropic: Serotonin syndrome)

explanation.summary: Serotonin syndrome is recognised by the triad of neuromuscular excitability (clonus, hyperreflexia, tremor), autonomic instability (tachycardia, hypertension, hyperthermia, mydriasis) and altered mental status, in the setting of serotonergic drug exposure. Tramadol combined with SSRIs is a common trigger. Management is drug cessation, IV fluids, benzodiazepines, active cooling and cyproheptadine if severe.
key_points: Hunter criteria identify serotonin syndrome (clonus, hyperreflexia, tremor)
Tramadol plus SSRI is a high-risk combination
Differentiate from NMS by rigidity and rate of onset
explanation.pearls: NMS develops over days to weeks with rigidity and bradyreflexia; serotonin syndrome develops over hours with clonus and hyperreflexia; getting this distinction right changes management to dantrolene/bromocriptine in NMS or cyproheptadine in serotonin syndrome.

stem: A 28-year-old woman presents to her GP with 8 months of excessive worry about work, family, finances and her own health. She describes restlessness, fatigue, irritability, muscle tension and insomnia most nights. She has had 2 episodes of panic in the last month. Her mood is anxious but she denies persistent low mood, anhedonia or thoughts of self-harm. She uses no substances. GAD-7 score is 16.

data_table.Examination: Vital signs within normal limits; mental state: anxious affect, normal speech, no psychotic features, no suicidal ideation; cognition intact
data_table.Investigations: TSH 1.8, full blood count and electrolytes normal; urine drug screen negative; ECG normal

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate initial management?

options.text: Benzodiazepine diazepam 5 mg three times daily for 6 weeks as first-line monotherapy
options.rationale: Benzodiazepines are not first-line in GAD due to tolerance, dependence and impaired psychomotor function; SSRIs or SNRIs and CBT are preferred. (Source: eTG Psychotropic: Generalised anxiety disorder)

options.text: Sertraline 50 mg daily titrating to 100-200 mg with referral for cognitive behavioural therapy
options.rationale: An SSRI such as sertraline combined with cognitive behavioural therapy is first-line treatment for GAD per Australian guidelines; expected onset of benefit is 4 to 6 weeks. (Source: eTG Psychotropic: Generalised anxiety disorder)

options.text: Olanzapine 5 mg daily as augmentation for severe anxiety symptoms ongoing despite GP review
options.rationale: Antipsychotics are not first-line in GAD and carry significant metabolic and motor side effects. (Source: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guideline: Anxiety disorders)
source_refs: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guideline: Anxiety disorders

options.text: Beta-blocker propranolol 40 mg three times daily as first-line monotherapy for somatic symptoms
options.rationale: Propranolol may help performance anxiety but is not first-line for GAD where SSRIs and CBT address core cognitive and worry symptoms. (Source: eTG Psychotropic: Generalised anxiety disorder)

options.text: Refer for inpatient psychiatric admission given the severity score on GAD-7 questionnaire
options.rationale: GAD-7 of 16 is severe but does not by itself indicate admission; outpatient pharmacotherapy and psychotherapy is appropriate given employment and absence of suicidality. (Source: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guideline: Anxiety disorders)
source_refs: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guideline: Anxiety disorders

explanation.summary: First-line treatment of moderate to severe generalised anxiety disorder is an SSRI or SNRI combined with cognitive behavioural therapy. Benzodiazepines are limited to short-term use due to dependence risk. Antipsychotics and beta-blockers are not first-line. Treatment effect for SSRIs is typically seen at 4 to 6 weeks with dose titration as needed.
key_points: SSRI plus CBT is first-line for moderate-severe GAD
Benzodiazepines are short-term adjuncts only due to dependence risk
Treatment response is typically assessed at 4-6 weeks
explanation.pearls: Counsel patients about possible initial worsening of anxiety in the first 1-2 weeks of SSRI initiation; lower starting doses (sertraline 25 mg) may reduce activation symptoms.

sources.label: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guideline: Anxiety disorders

stem: A 24-year-old man with treatment-resistant schizophrenia is on day 18 of clozapine titration, currently at 175 mg daily. He has been responding well to treatment but today presents to the emergency department with fever, malaise, persistent tachycardia and mild dyspnoea on exertion. He denies cough, sputum or urinary symptoms. He has no source of infection on examination.

data_table.Vital signs: Temp 38.7 degrees Celsius, HR 120 sinus, BP 110/68, RR 22, SpO2 96% in room air
data_table.Examination: No focus of infection on ENT or chest examination; chest clear; heart sounds soft with no murmur; abdomen soft; no rash
data_table.Investigations: Troponin I 250 ng/L (less than 14), ECG: sinus tachycardia with non-specific T wave flattening, CXR: clear, WCC 9.5 with no left shift, CRP 60, BNP 200, blood cultures sent

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate immediate action?

options.text: Continue clozapine and treat symptomatically with paracetamol given non-specific viral picture
options.rationale: Persistent tachycardia, fever and elevated troponin in the first 4 weeks of clozapine titration is suggestive of clozapine-induced myocarditis and requires immediate cessation. (Source: RANZCP Clozapine treatment guidelines)

options.text: Reduce clozapine dose by 50 percent and recheck the troponin level in 48 hours review
options.rationale: Dose reduction is inadequate when myocarditis is suspected; immediate cessation and urgent cardiac assessment are required given the mortality risk. (Source: RANZCP Clozapine treatment guidelines)

options.text: Cease clozapine immediately, admit for cardiac assessment with echocardiogram, and never rechallenge
options.rationale: Clozapine-induced myocarditis presents in the first 4 weeks of titration with tachycardia, fever and elevated troponin; immediate cessation, cardiology referral with echocardiogram, and avoidance of any future clozapine rechallenge is required. (Source: RANZCP Clozapine treatment guidelines)

options.text: Add propranolol 40 mg three times daily for tachycardia and continue clozapine titration as planned
options.rationale: Symptomatic beta-blockade does not address the underlying myocarditis and continued exposure increases mortality risk. (Source: RANZCP Clozapine treatment guidelines)

options.text: Switch directly to olanzapine 20 mg daily and discharge with outpatient cardiology follow-up review
options.rationale: Outpatient management is unsafe in suspected myocarditis; admission for cardiac assessment is required before any switch is made. (Source: eTG Psychotropic: Clozapine)

explanation.summary: Clozapine-induced myocarditis typically presents in the first 4 weeks of titration with persistent tachycardia, fever, elevated troponin and constitutional symptoms. Mandatory monitoring includes baseline and weekly CRP, troponin and FBC for the first 4 weeks. Suspected myocarditis requires immediate clozapine cessation, urgent cardiac assessment with echocardiogram, and lifelong avoidance of rechallenge.
key_points: Suspect clozapine myocarditis in weeks 1-4 with fever, tachycardia and high troponin
Immediate cessation and cardiac assessment is mandatory
Rechallenge with clozapine is contraindicated after myocarditis
explanation.pearls: Monitoring during titration includes baseline echocardiogram, weekly CRP and troponin for 4 weeks, and weekly FBC for 18 weeks then monthly for the duration of treatment.

stem: A 26-year-old woman on risperidone 4 mg daily for 6 months for schizophrenia has had a good symptomatic response. She presents to the early psychosis clinic with 4 months of amenorrhoea, milk discharge from both breasts and reduced libido. She wishes to retain antipsychotic efficacy. She is sexually active and uses condoms; pregnancy test is negative.

data_table.Examination: Breast examination: bilateral expressible non-bloody galactorrhoea, no palpable mass; visual fields full to confrontation; mental state stable with no psychotic relapse features
data_table.Investigations: Prolactin 1800 mIU/L (less than 500), TSH 1.6, beta-hCG negative, FSH and LH suppressed; MRI pituitary normal; LFTs and renal function normal

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Add cabergoline 0.25 mg twice weekly to reduce prolactin while continuing risperidone unchanged
options.rationale: Dopamine agonists may precipitate psychotic relapse in schizophrenia and are not the preferred approach when an alternative antipsychotic option exists. (Source: eTG Psychotropic: Antipsychotic adverse effects)

options.text: Stop risperidone and commence oral oestradiol-progestogen combined contraceptive only
options.rationale: Stopping the antipsychotic without substitution risks psychotic relapse; hormonal therapy alone does not address the underlying dopamine D2 blockade. (Source: AMH: Risperidone)

options.text: Reduce risperidone to 2 mg and add bromocriptine 2.5 mg daily as combination management
options.rationale: Combination dopamine agonist plus antipsychotic risks psychotic relapse and is less preferred than switching to a prolactin-sparing agent. (Source: eTG Psychotropic: Antipsychotic adverse effects)

options.text: Switch from risperidone to aripiprazole and titrate to symptom control over 4-6 weeks
options.rationale: Risperidone is a strong dopamine D2 antagonist causing prolactin elevation; aripiprazole is a partial dopamine D2 agonist and is prolactin-sparing, making it the preferred switch when antipsychotic-induced hyperprolactinaemia is symptomatic. (Source: eTG Psychotropic: Antipsychotic adverse effects)

options.text: Continue risperidone and reassure as hyperprolactinaemia is asymptomatic and benign here
options.rationale: The patient is symptomatic with amenorrhoea, galactorrhoea and reduced libido; long-term hyperprolactinaemia also raises concerns about hypogonadism and reduced bone density. (Source: AMH: Risperidone)

explanation.summary: Symptomatic antipsychotic-induced hyperprolactinaemia is best managed by switching to a prolactin-sparing antipsychotic such as aripiprazole, which is a partial D2 agonist. Cabergoline carries a risk of precipitating psychotic relapse. Long-term untreated hyperprolactinaemia leads to hypogonadism and reduced bone mineral density.
key_points: Aripiprazole is prolactin-sparing due to partial D2 agonism
Risperidone, paliperidone and amisulpride are high-prolactin agents
Untreated hyperprolactinaemia causes hypogonadism and bone loss
explanation.pearls: Olanzapine, quetiapine and clozapine are also relatively prolactin-sparing options if aripiprazole is unsuitable; baseline prolactin should be checked before commencing any antipsychotic with high prolactin liability.

subtopic_detail: Severe anorexia nervosa medical admission criteria
stem: A 19-year-old female university student presents to her GP with 8 months of progressive restrictive eating and 30 percent weight loss from baseline. She has weakness, dizziness on standing and feels cold. She denies binging or purging. Mental state shows intense fear of weight gain and distorted body image. She does not feel suicidal.

data_table.Vital signs: BMI 12.8 kg/m2; HR 38 sinus, BP 85/55 supine and 70/40 standing, temp 35.6 degrees Celsius
data_table.Examination: Cachectic, lanugo hair, cold peripheries, slow capillary refill 3 seconds, proximal weakness on squat test; abdomen soft and non-tender
data_table.Investigations: K 2.8, PO4 0.6, Mg 0.6, Na 134, glucose 3.2, urea 4.2, creatinine 50, LFTs ALT 90 AST 110, albumin 28; ECG sinus bradycardia QTc 470 ms

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate disposition and initial management?

options.text: Refer to specialist outpatient eating disorder team with weekly weights and dietitian reviews scheduled
options.rationale: Outpatient management is unsafe when patients meet MARSIPAN medical instability criteria; inpatient medical admission is required. (Source: RANZCP MARSIPAN guidance: Management of really sick patients with anorexia nervosa)
source_refs: RANZCP MARSIPAN guidance: Management of really sick patients with anorexia nervosa

options.text: Discharge with family-based therapy referral and weekly GP review for weight monitoring at home
options.rationale: FBT is an appropriate longer-term therapy but does not address acute medical instability which requires immediate medical admission. (Source: ANZAED: Eating disorder medical management)

options.text: Admit to a general adult ward with a standard three-meal regimen at full calories from day one
options.rationale: Full caloric refeeding from day 1 in severe anorexia nervosa risks refeeding syndrome including hypophosphataemia, cardiac arrhythmia and Wernicke encephalopathy. (Source: RANZCP MARSIPAN guidance: Management of really sick patients with anorexia nervosa)
source_refs: RANZCP MARSIPAN guidance: Management of really sick patients with anorexia nervosa

options.text: Cognitive behavioural therapy for eating disorders intensive outpatient program with weekly therapy
options.rationale: CBT-ED is an evidence-based outpatient therapy in less severe disease; it is not appropriate when MARSIPAN criteria for medical instability are met. (Source: ANZAED: Eating disorder medical management)

options.text: Admit to medical ward with cardiac monitoring, electrolyte correction and slow refeeding with daily monitoring
options.rationale: MARSIPAN medical instability criteria (BMI under 13, HR under 40, postural BP drop above 20, hypothermia, K under 3.0, hypoglycaemia, prolonged QTc) require medical admission with cardiac monitoring, slow refeeding from 5-10 kcal/kg/day, electrolyte correction and daily monitoring for refeeding syndrome. (Source: RANZCP MARSIPAN guidance: Management of really sick patients with anorexia nervosa)
source_refs: RANZCP MARSIPAN guidance: Management of really sick patients with anorexia nervosa

explanation.summary: Severe anorexia nervosa meeting MARSIPAN medical instability criteria requires medical admission with continuous cardiac monitoring, slow refeeding from 5-10 kcal/kg/day, electrolyte and thiamine supplementation, daily bloods and clinical review for refeeding syndrome. Outpatient and standard ward management without monitoring are unsafe.
key_points: MARSIPAN criteria identify need for medical admission in severe AN
Refeeding starts at 5-10 kcal/kg/day with daily phosphate monitoring
Cardiac monitoring is required for QTc prolongation or bradycardia
explanation.pearls: Family-based therapy, CBT-ED and specialist outpatient eating disorder programs are evidence-based long-term treatments but only after medical stabilisation; involve mental health services early to plan post-admission care.

sources.label: RANZCP MARSIPAN guidance: Management of really sick patients with anorexia nervosa
